content.text: Based on the official Claude Code documentation,
"Gathering Context"
is the
first phase of the agentic loop
(also called "The Agent Loop") in Claude. Here's what it means:
What It Is
When you give Claude a task, it works through three phases:
gather context → take action → verify results
. "Gathering Context" is where Claude understands your task by retrieving relevant information before making changes.
Key Activities in This Phase
Activity
What It Does
Use subagents
Spawn parallel subagents for task efficiency to delegate work
Compact/maintain context
Summarize and compress message history to preserve critical details
Agentic/semantic search
Search files and codebases to retrieve relevant context for the task
Read files
Explore your project by reading multiple files to understand how components connect
Why It Matters
Gathering Context is essential because:
Claude sees your whole project
— unlike inline assistants that only see the current file, Claude can search across your entire directory and subdirectories
It informs next steps
— Each tool use during context gathering gives Claude new information that determines what action to take next
Token efficiency
— More context means lower quality code if you spend too many tokens per-loop; subagents help by returning summaries instead of bloating your main context
Mental Model
Think of it like a developer debugging: before fixing code, you first
read the error output
search for relevant files
, and
understand the codebase
. Claude does this autonomously using tools like file reading, search, and execution.
Key Takeaways
Gathering Context is phase 1 of Claude's 3-phase agentic loop
Uses subagents, semantic search, and context compaction to retrieve relevant information
These phases blend together — Claude uses tools throughout all phases
The loop adapts based on what Claude learns from each previous step

attrs.alt: Context Gathering Phase: Tools and Information Flow

content.text: Context Gathering in Depth

The gather-context phase is where Claude builds its internal model of your project before making any changes. This phase is fundamentally about reducing uncertainty: Claude doesn't know your codebase, your conventions, your dependencies, or where the relevant code lives. Every tool call during this phase narrows the search space and builds a richer mental model.

The quality of the context-gathering phase directly determines the quality of subsequent actions. An agent that rushes past context gathering and makes assumptions will produce incorrect diffs, miss dependencies, break unrelated code, and require multiple correction cycles. An agent that invests properly in understanding the codebase before acting will make targeted, correct changes on the first attempt.
The Three Context-Gathering Tools in Detail
Claude Code uses three primary read-only tools during context gathering: Glob, Grep, and Read. Each serves a distinct purpose and they are used in a specific sequence depending on the task.
Glob — file discovery:

Before reading any file, Claude needs to know which files exist. Glob performs fast pattern-based file discovery (e.g., `**/*.ts`, `src/**/*.test.js`). It answers: where are the relevant files? This is the outermost layer of context — the shape of the project.
Grep — content discovery:

Once Claude knows which files exist, it searches for relevant symbols, function names, import patterns, or configuration keys across the codebase. Grep with regex patterns helps Claude find where a specific function is defined, all callers of an API, or all files that import a module.
Read — file comprehension:

After identifying the right files, Claude reads them completely. This gives it the actual code — function signatures, implementation logic, type definitions, test expectations. Read is the deepest and most token-expensive operation, so Claude uses Glob and Grep to narrow the target set first.
Parallel Context Gathering with Subagents

For large codebases, a single-agent sequential approach to context gathering can be slow and token-expensive. Claude Code supports spawning subagents during the context phase to parallelize information retrieval. Each subagent runs in isolation with its own context window, performs a specific exploration task, and returns a summary to the main agent.

This pattern is especially valuable when gathering context that spans independent concerns: one subagent can explore the database schema while another reads API endpoint definitions, and a third examines the test suite. The orchestrating agent receives three clean summaries instead of one bloated context window.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def gather_context_parallel(repo_root: str, task_description: str) -> dict:
    """
    Use parallel subagents to gather context from multiple codebase layers.
    Each subagent focuses on one concern and returns a compact summary.
    """
    
    # Define independent context-gathering tasks
    context_tasks = [
        {
            "focus": "API surface",
            "instruction": f"Explore {repo_root}/src/api/** and summarize: "
                          f"all route handlers, their inputs/outputs, middleware used. "
                          f"Max 200 words. Task context: {task_description}"
        },
        {
            "focus": "Data models",  
            "instruction": f"Explore {repo_root}/src/models/** and {repo_root}/prisma/schema.prisma. "
                          f"Summarize all entities, key fields, relationships. Max 200 words."
        },
        {
            "focus": "Test patterns",
            "instruction": f"Read 3 test files from {repo_root}/tests/. "
                          f"Summarize testing conventions, fixtures used, assertion patterns. Max 150 words."
        },
    ]
    
    gathered = {}
    
    # In production, these run concurrently via async/threading
    for task in context_tasks:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-5",
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task["instruction"]}],
        )
        gathered[task["focus"]] = response.content[0].text
    
    return gathered

# Example usage
context = gather_context_parallel(
    repo_root="/home/user/myproject",
    task_description="Add user authentication with JWT tokens"
)
for focus, summary in context.items():
    print(f"=== {focus} ===")
    print(summary)
    print()
Context Window Management During Gathering

Context accumulates throughout the gathering phase. Every file read, every grep result, every subagent summary adds tokens to the context window. Claude Code must balance comprehensiveness (reading everything) with token efficiency (only reading what matters).
Targeted reads over full directory scans:
Claude reads specific files identified by Glob/Grep rather than every file in a directory.
Summarization at the edge:
When spawning subagents for context, each subagent returns a compact summary rather than raw file contents. This prevents the main context from bloating with irrelevant detail.
Context compaction:

Claude Code monitors context window usage and can compact earlier conversation turns into summaries while preserving critical facts. The compaction mechanism identifies which parts of the history are no longer needed for current reasoning.
Priority ordering:

Claude reads high-signal files first — entry points, configuration, tests — before diving into implementation details. This ensures that if context fills up, the most important information is already loaded.
What Claude Actually Collects
The output of the context-gathering phase is not a formal data structure — it is a rich, implicit understanding encoded in Claude's context window. This understanding typically includes:
Project structure — directory layout, module boundaries, entry points
Dependency graph — which modules import which, which functions call which
Existing patterns — naming conventions, error handling patterns, test structure
Relevant code — the specific functions, types, and data structures involved in the task
Configuration — environment variables, build configuration, feature flags
Test coverage — which code paths have tests and what those tests expect
API contracts — interface definitions, input/output shapes, error cases
Common Context-Gathering Patterns
Bottom-Up: Follow the Error

When fixing a bug, Claude starts from the error message or failing test and traces back through the call stack. It reads the failing test, then the function it tests, then the dependencies of that function, expanding outward until it has a complete picture of the bug's context.

# Pattern: trace from symptom to root cause
# Step 1: Read the failing test
# grep for test name, read the test file
# Step 2: Identify the function under test
# Step 3: Read that function's implementation
# Step 4: Identify suspicious code paths
# Step 5: Read dependencies of the suspicious path
# Step 6: Locate the bug source
# Now ready to take action with full context

def context_gather_bug_fix(error_message: str, test_file: str) -> list[str]:
    """Returns ordered list of files to read for bug-fix context."""
    reading_order = []
    
    # Always start with the failing test
    reading_order.append(test_file)
    
    # Parse error message to find module path
    # e.g., "TypeError in auth/tokens.py line 42"
    if "in " in error_message and ".py" in error_message:
        module = error_message.split("in ")[1].split(" ")[0]
        reading_order.append(module)
    
    # Read imports of that module to find dependencies
    # (in real usage, Claude reads the file and follows imports)
    return reading_order
Top-Down: Feature Addition

When adding a new feature, Claude starts from the project's entry points and works downward toward where the new code should live. It reads the routing layer to understand the URL structure, then the controller layer for request handling patterns, then the service layer for business logic, then the data layer for persistence patterns.
Architecture & Implementation: Semantic Search

In addition to syntactic pattern matching with Glob and Grep, Claude Code performs semantic search by asking the right questions in the right order. Unlike vector-similarity-based semantic search, this is inference-driven: Claude reasons about where relevant code would be located given what it knows about the project's architecture.

For example, given a task to "add rate limiting to the API", Claude reasons: rate limiting typically lives at the middleware layer, middleware is usually near the entry point, entry points in Express apps are typically in index.js or app.js or server.js. It then Globs for those files and reads them to confirm the project structure before searching for existing middleware patterns.
Architecture inference: Claude uses knowledge of common project structures to predict file locations
Convention following: Claude reads 1-2 existing implementations before adding a new one to learn the project's style
Dependency awareness: Claude identifies not just what files to read but in what order, following the dependency graph
Negative space: Claude also gathers context about what does NOT exist — missing tests, absent configuration, unimplemented interfaces
Common Pitfalls in Context Gathering
Reading too much too early:
Loading entire directories instead of targeting relevant files wastes tokens and dilutes the context with irrelevant information.
Skipping context gathering entirely:
Jumping straight to action without understanding the codebase leads to changes that break other components.
Not reading the tests:
Tests are the most precise specification of expected behavior. Skipping them during context gathering leads to implementations that pass the wrong tests or no tests.
Ignoring configuration:
Many bugs stem from incorrect configuration. Not reading config files leaves Claude with an incomplete picture.
Over-relying on file names:
File names are hints, not specifications. Always read the file to confirm it contains what you expect.
Forgetting to check for existing implementations:
Before implementing a utility function, Claude should check whether it already exists to avoid duplication.
Best Practices for Effective Context Gathering
Always read tests before reading implementations — tests define the contract
Use Glob with specific patterns rather than listing entire directories
Read entry points first to understand the application's shape
Follow import chains when debugging to trace the dependency graph
Spawn subagents for independent concerns to parallelize gathering
Read error messages and stack traces completely before searching for causes
Check for configuration files (package.json, pyproject.toml, .env.example) early
Note what is absent as well as what is present — missing tests matter
Frequently Asked Questions
How does Claude decide when enough context has been gathered?

Claude stops gathering context when it can construct a complete mental model of what needs to change and why. Concretely: it knows which files to edit, it understands the existing patterns to follow, it has read the tests that will verify its changes, and it can predict the likely side effects of its planned changes. If any of these elements is missing, it gathers more context.
Can context gathering itself fail?

Yes. If the project structure is unusual, if files are named counterintuitively, or if the task requires understanding a domain-specific abstraction that Claude hasn't encountered, context gathering may produce an incomplete picture. The verification phase catches these failures: if the action taken based on incomplete context produces a failing test or a lint error, Claude loops back to gather more context before trying again.
How does context compaction affect gathering?

Context compaction summarizes earlier parts of the conversation to free up space for new information. During long context-gathering sessions, compaction may summarize early file reads. This is why Claude is designed to extract the key facts it needs immediately rather than relying on being able to refer back to raw file contents later.
What is the difference between gathering context and remembering context?

Gathering context is an active process within a single agentic loop iteration — Claude reads files and searches the codebase to build understanding for the current task. Remembering context refers to persisted memory (CLAUDE.md, transcript history, memory directory) that carries information across sessions. Context gathered within a session is ephemeral; it exists only while the conversation continues.
